evidence: Package-level globals (`contractCatalog`, `actionContractBindings`, `actionContractBindingsByKey`) are populated via `contractRegistryInitErr = initContractRegistry()` at import time.
The package `init()` panics when registry validation fails, making any importer transitively fail process startup (`panic(fmt.Sprintf("invalid protocol contract registry: %v", ...))`).
Registry construction, validation, storage, and read APIs are all concentrated in one module, creating a central coordination hub for protocol actions and error contracts.

Multiple modules model dependencies as large function-pointer bags with per-field nil checks and forwarding methods (e.g., importerRuntime in goseg/importer/importer.go, workflowRuntime in goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go, startupInitRuntime in goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go).

Orchestration defines a broad lattice of embedded runtime structs (RuntimeContainerOps/RuntimeUrbitOps/Runtime*Ops) while adjacent packages also define concrete runtimes with overlapping capabilities (goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go vs goseg/docker/network/network.go and goseg/docker/lifecycle/planner.go).

Configuration abstraction is a wide mutable bag model: SysConfig and UrbitDocker aggregate many concerns (goseg/structs/configs.go), and ConfPatch plus ConfUpdateOption exposes a large optional-field patch surface (goseg/config/config_update_schema.go), encouraging callers to operate on coarse-grained config objects rather than focused contracts.

The orchestration API layer repeats a validate-and-delegate shape across many top-level functions (e.g., `StartContainer`, `CreateContainer`, `StopContainerByName`, `RestartContainer`, `ExecDockerCommand`) while also duplicating runtime and non-runtime variants, creating a broad pass-through surface with little behavioral variation (`goseg/docker/orchestration/orchestration.go`).

Runtime composition in orchestration is fragmented across multiple large seam structs and builder/option functions (`Runtime`, `RuntimeTransitionOps`, `RuntimeHealthOps`, `RuntimeStartupOps`, many `With...` options), making change paths span multiple files before reaching behavior (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_docker.go`).

Session/auth boundaries are blurred by wrapper-heavy forwarding APIs: `auth` forwards to `session`/`tokens`, while `session` contains large delegating method sets over internal state objects, increasing indirection without consolidating domain ownership (`goseg/auth/auth.go`, `goseg/session/session.go`).

System concerns are spread across disk management, wifi control loops, telemetry, and host cron/fixer setup in the same package area, indicating mixed responsibilities at package-level cohesion (`goseg/system/system.go`, `goseg/system/disk.go`, `goseg/system/wifi.go`).

Test files repeatedly implement bespoke global seam reset scaffolding (save globals, override, restore in cleanup), signaling absence of a shared test harness pattern and structural duplication in test architecture (`goseg/system/disk_test.go`, `goseg/startram/state_sync_test.go`, `goseg/logger/logger_test.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_test.go`).

findings.summary: Contract registry validation runs at import time and can panic, coupling all importers to this package's startup side effects.
evidence: `var contractRegistryInitErr = initContractRegistry()` executes during package initialization.
`init()` panics if `contractRegistryInitErr != nil`, so consumers cannot handle contract-registry failures explicitly.
Public functions (for example `ValidateActionContractBindings`) still check `contractRegistryInitErr`, indicating mixed startup and runtime error paths.
findings.suggestion: Remove panic-driven init and expose an explicit bootstrap/constructor (for example `NewRegistry` or `LoadRegistry`) that returns errors; have callers wire startup validation in composition roots instead of import-time side effects.

findings.summary: A single file acts as the protocol governance hub (IDs, metadata policy, binding map, version semantics), increasing blast radius of contract changes.
evidence: The same module defines contract IDs, action tokens, lifecycle metadata schema, compatibility validation rules, version parsing/comparison helpers, and runtime lookup APIs.
Adding or changing any contract requires editing centralized entry lists and per-contract version constants in this file.
The consolidated `entries` table mixes multiple domains (upload actions, c2c actions, and StarTram API error contracts) into one registry path.
findings.suggestion: Split registry ownership by bounded context (for example upload/c2c/startram contract providers) and compose them through a small registry interface; keep shared validation in a narrow core package to reduce cross-domain coupling.

findings.summary: Function-pointer seam bags add heavy indirection and boilerplate without proportional behavioral leverage.
evidence: importerRuntime defines many callbacks, then wraps most in guard/forwarder methods (e.g., updateContainerState, waitForBootCode, waitForRemoteReady), duplicating policy-free plumbing.
workflowRuntime similarly carries many fn fields and is repeatedly normalized via withDefaultsWorkflowRuntime before use, indicating call sites cannot rely on a coherent runtime contract.
startupInitRuntime and its withDefaults pattern repeat nil-merging logic across many startup callbacks, and tests replicate adapter scaffolding to construct partial runtimes.
findings.suggestion: Replace broad function-pointer bags with smaller capability interfaces and concrete adapters per workflow phase (startup/import/transition). Move nil/default handling to constructor boundaries so runtime objects are always valid post-construction.

findings.summary: Parallel runtime abstractions in orchestration/network/lifecycle duplicate responsibility and blur dependency boundaries.
evidence: RuntimeVolumeOps/RuntimeContainerNetworkOps in orchestration overlap with concrete methods on network.NetworkRuntime (CreateVolume, VolumeExists, AddOrGetNetwork).

Lifecycle planning resolves image/config/startup through its own Runtime/containerPlan path while orchestration maintains separate runtime seam bundles, creating two abstraction families for similar container orchestration concerns.
Wireguard runtime wiring composes many seam callbacks and pass-through selectors (e.g., SelectImageFn, EnsureVolumesFn), further stacking indirection across these overlapping contracts.
findings.suggestion: Define a single orchestration boundary per concern (container lifecycle, network/volume, config artifacts) and have lifecycle/network modules implement those shared contracts directly; remove duplicate runtime-op layers where no policy transformation is added.

findings.summary: Monolithic config and patch bags hide domain boundaries and force broad coupling.
evidence: SysConfig aggregates unrelated concerns (auth/session, networking, update policy, swap, penpai, startram, backup), making it a catch-all transport model.
ConfPatch mirrors this with a large optional-field patch bag and a registry dispatcher, so updates are modeled as coarse-grained mutation packets rather than bounded domain commands.
Startup/default wiring pulls broad snapshots and default structs from this shared bag model, reinforcing cross-cutting dependency on a large mutable configuration surface.
findings.suggestion: Split SysConfig/ConfPatch into domain-scoped config aggregates (e.g., connectivity, startram, runtime resources, auth/session) with dedicated update APIs per aggregate; keep translation to legacy JSON at the persistence edge.

findings.summary: Contract catalog validation runs at import time and can panic the process across modules.
evidence: contracts.go computes `contractRegistryInitErr = initContractRegistry()` as a package-global side effect and `init()` panics if non-nil.
README documents contract governance as a quality gate concern, but this implementation enforces correctness via boot-time crash instead of explicit startup validation boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Replace import-time registry construction with explicit startup initialization (or lazy once-init returning errors) and require callers to handle a returned contract-registry error at composition boundaries.

findings.summary: A single contract registry file owns both protocol-action and StarTram error contracts.
evidence: contracts.go defines IDs for upload/C2C actions and StarTram API connection error in one `entries` catalog.

README describes separate governance surfaces across `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` and `goseg/startram/errors.go`, indicating distinct module boundaries that are currently centralized in one shared hub.
findings.suggestion: Split registry ownership by subsystem (e.g., per-package contract declarations with shared schema types only in `contracts`), then compose a federated catalog through explicit registration APIs to reduce hub-module blast radius.

findings.summary: Runtime APIs still accept raw namespace/action strings, weakening documented contract governance.
evidence: contracts.go exports raw action token constants and a stringly API `ActionContractDescriptor(namespace, action string)`.
README requires descriptor-centric compatibility governance across modules, but raw string entry points let callers avoid descriptor IDs and type-safe bindings.
findings.suggestion: Promote `ContractID`/`ActionContractBinding` as the only public lookup path (typed namespace/action or ID-only APIs), and deprecate raw string lookup to enforce cross-module contract compatibility policy consistently.

findings.summary: Orchestration exposes a large repeated wrapper surface where many functions only validate inputs and delegate, duplicating structural flow across runtime and package APIs.
evidence: `orchestration.go` repeats the same pattern in multiple entry points: validate input, then call runtime method (`StartContainer`, `CreateContainer`, `DeleteContainer`, `RestartContainer`, `ExecDockerCommand`, etc.).
Runtime projections in `runtime_core.go` (e.g., `NewDockerTransitionRuntime`, `NewDockerHealthRuntime`) manually copy many function fields, reinforcing duplicated wiring logic.
The ops decomposition in `runtime_ops.go` spreads behavior contracts across many nested structs, requiring parallel wrapper methods rather than a smaller cohesive command surface.
findings.suggestion: Collapse wrapper-heavy APIs into a smaller command-oriented orchestration service: centralize validation in shared helpers/interceptors, keep one public execution path per operation type, and generate or table-drive repetitive runtime field projections to remove manual duplicated delegation.

findings.summary: Auth and session layers duplicate gateway responsibilities via pass-through methods, weakening module boundaries and increasing indirection cost.
evidence: `auth/auth.go` includes many thin wrappers that primarily guard args then forward to `session`, `authsession`, or `tokens`.
`session/session.go` includes extensive delegating methods in `logstreamRuntime` that mostly forward to `state.logstream` with repeated nil checks.
Both modules expose broad procedural accessors/mutators instead of enforcing a narrower cohesive boundary for authentication/session ownership.
findings.suggestion: Define explicit ownership boundaries: keep auth policy/token logic in `auth`, keep connection/session lifecycle in `session`, and replace repeated wrappers with focused interfaces that expose only behavior actually needed by callers.

findings.summary: System package combines unrelated host management concerns (telemetry, cron bootstrap, disk provisioning, wifi state machine), reducing cohesion.
evidence: `system.go` mixes runtime metrics (`GetMemory`, `GetCPU`, `GetDisk`, `GetTemp`) with installer/ops behavior (`FixerScript`, `addCron`, `cronExists`) and shell command utilities.
`disk.go` carries filesystem provisioning, SMART checks, mount/fstab reconciliation, and multiple command seams in one unit.
`wifi.go` implements interface discovery, connectivity actions, captive rules, and lifecycle loop globals in the same package scope.
findings.suggestion: Split `system` into cohesive submodules (e.g., `system/metrics`, `system/bootstrap`, `system/storage`, `system/wifi`) with narrow interfaces and explicit orchestration points, so each file/module maps to one operational responsibility.

findings.summary: Tests repeatedly hand-roll global seam save/override/restore logic, indicating a missing shared testing pattern and duplicated structure.
evidence: `disk_test.go` defines `resetDiskSeams` to snapshot and restore many globals manually.
`state_sync_test.go` repeatedly snapshots/restores package globals (`defaultAPIClient`, `defaultConfigService`, `defaultRetrieveStateSyncer`) in individual tests.
`logger_test.go` defines multiple reset helpers for sink/global init state; `wifi_test.go` carries custom runtime assembly helpers with minimal shared harness.
findings.suggestion: Introduce package-level test harness helpers (or a shared internal testutil module) for seam override lifecycle, so tests declare only scenario-specific overrides and reuse one consistent cleanup mechanism.
